verses.text: 22:1-51 Although this prayer of thanksgiving (also recorded in Ps 18) is placed near the end of David’s story, David probably offered it to God much earlier in his life. This prayer and Hannah’s (1 Sam 2:1-10) together enclose the book of Samuel with an inclusio (literary bookends). Hannah was saved from barrenness; David was saved from his enemies. The placement of this hymn also provides a parallel to Moses. The stories of both Moses and David end with a song or hymn giving lavish praise to God (see also Deut 31:30–32:43). Both highlight God as a “Rock” (Deut 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31; 2 Sam 22:2, 3, 32, 47). Both are followed by second and shorter poetic pieces—Moses’ final blessing to the Israelite tribes (Deut 33), and David’s last words (2 Sam 23:1-7).

22:2 Hannah had said, “there is no Rock like our God” (1 Sam 2:2), while David said, the Lord is my rock, which recalls God’s rescue of David from Saul at the Rock of Escape (1 Sam 23:28). The Hebrew word translated “rock” in 22:3 (different from 22:2) refers in 1 Sam 24:2 to “the rocks of the wild goats,” where Saul suspected that David was hiding. • fortress: The same word referred to David’s physical “stronghold” (1 Sam 22:4), where David and his men sought refuge from Saul.
22:3 The phrase the power that saves me (literally the horn that has saved me) is similar to Hannah’s “the Lord has made me strong!,” (literally the Lord has exalted my horn, 1 Sam 2:1).

22:4 The sorrows of hell compassed me about; the snares of death prevented me. See Gill on Ps 18:5. . 2 Samuel 22:7 sa2 22:7 sa2 22:7 sa2 22:7In my distress I called upon the Lord, and cried to my God: and he did hear my voice out of his temple, and my cry did enter into his ears. See Gill on Ps 18:6.
22:5 When the waves of death compassed me - Though in a primary sense many of these things belong to David, yet generally and fully they belong to the Messiah alone.

22:6 He bowed the heavens also, and came down; and darkness was under his feet. See Gill on Ps 18:9. . 2 Samuel 22:11 sa2 22:11 sa2 22:11 sa2 22:11And he rode upon a cherub, and did fly: and he was seen upon the wings of the wind. See Gill on Ps 18:10.
22:7 Although his sanctuary sometimes refers to the Tabernacle or Temple, here it refers to God’s heavenly dwelling; neither the Tabernacle nor the Temple were in existence at this time.

22:8-20 David vividly expands on how God heard him (22:7). David describes God’s rescue as a theophany (manifestation of God’s presence; see study notes on Exod 19:16-25; Deut 1:33) that recalls God’s manifestation at Sinai (Exod 19:16-20; cp. Judg 5:4-5).

22:8-20 David vividly expands on how God heard him (22:7). David describes God’s rescue as a theophany (manifestation of God’s presence; see study notes on Exod 19:16-25; Deut 1:33) that recalls God’s manifestation at Sinai (Exod 19:16-20; cp. Judg 5:4-5).
22:14 The Lord thundered from heaven: Cp. 1 Sam 2:10.

22:15 With the pure thou wilt show thyself pure; and with the froward thou wilt show thyself unsavoury. See Gill on Ps 18:26. . 2 Samuel 22:28 sa2 22:28 sa2 22:28 sa2 22:28And the afflicted people thou wilt save: but thine eyes are upon the haughty, that thou mayest bring them down. See Gill on Ps 18:27.

22:16 For thou art my lamp, O Lord: and the Lord will lighten my darkness. See Gill on Ps 18:28. . 2 Samuel 22:30 sa2 22:30 sa2 22:30 sa2 22:30For by thee I have run through a troop: by my God have I leaped over a wall. See Gill on Ps 18:29.

22:17 As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the Lord is tried: he is a buckler to all them that trust in him. See Gill on Ps 18:30. . 2 Samuel 22:32 sa2 22:32 sa2 22:32 sa2 22:32For who is God, save the Lord? and who is a rock, save our God? See Gill on Ps 18:31.

22:18 God is my strength and power: and he maketh my way perfect. See Gill on Ps 18:32. . 2 Samuel 22:34 sa2 22:34 sa2 22:34 sa2 22:34He maketh my feet like hinds' feet: and setteth me upon my high places. See Gill on Ps 18:33.

22:19 He teacheth my hands to war; so that a bow of steel is broken by mine arms. See Gill on Ps 18:34. . 2 Samuel 22:36 sa2 22:36 sa2 22:36 sa2 22:36Thou hast also given me the shield of thy salvation: and thy gentleness hath made me great. See Gill on Ps 18:35.

22:20 Thou hast enlarged my steps under me; so that my feet did not slip. See Gill on Ps 18:36. . 2 Samuel 22:38 sa2 22:38 sa2 22:38 sa2 22:38I have pursued mine enemies, and destroyed them; and turned not again until I had consumed them. See Gill on Ps 18:37.
22:21-30 David’s divine rescue and success were related to his own obedient walk with God. God honors those who order their lives in a way that pleases him (see also Lev 26:1-13; Deut 28:1-14; Ps 1).

22:22-24 David composed these words much earlier (22:1), before his sin with Bathsheba. Still, his claim to being blameless should not be construed as a claim to perfection. David was simply noting that he had kept the covenant and followed its law.

22:22-24 David composed these words much earlier (22:1), before his sin with Bathsheba. Still, his claim to being blameless should not be construed as a claim to perfection. David was simply noting that he had kept the covenant and followed its law.

22:22-24 David composed these words much earlier (22:1), before his sin with Bathsheba. Still, his claim to being blameless should not be construed as a claim to perfection. David was simply noting that he had kept the covenant and followed its law.

22:25 The Lord liveth; and blessed be my rock; and exalted be the God of the rock of my salvation. See Gill on Ps 18:46. . 2 Samuel 22:48 sa2 22:48 sa2 22:48 sa2 22:48It is God that avengeth me, and that bringeth down the people under me. See Gill on Ps 18:47.

22:26 And that bringeth me forth from mine enemies: thou also hast lifted me up on high above them that rose up against me: thou hast delivered me from the violent man. See Gill on Ps 18:48. . 2 Samuel 22:50 sa2 22:50 sa2 22:50 sa2 22:50Therefore I will give thanks unto thee, O Lord, among the heathen, and I will sing praises unto thy name. See Gill on Ps 18:49.
22:27 He is the tower of salvation for his king: and showeth mercy to his anointed, unto David, and to his seed for evermore. See Gill on Ps 18:50. Next: 2 Samuel Chapter 23

22:28 The afflicted people thou wilt save: but thine eyes are upon the haughty: A two-line theology of God's preferential look. *Ani* (afflicted) — the same word used in the Beatitudes' Greek echo (Matt 5:3-5; Luke 1:52-53). The Lord's eyes both elevate the lowly and bring down the lofty (Ps 18:27; Jas 4:6).

22:29 2 Sam 22:29 commences the description of the help which David had already received from God in his conflict with the enemies of Israel, and which he would still receive. 29 For Thou art my lamp, O Jehovah! And Jehovah maketh my darkness bright. 30 For through Thee I run troops, And through my God I leap walls. 31 God - innocent is His way. The word of Jehovah is refined, A shield is He to all who trust in Him.

22:30 In the strength of his God he could run hostile troops and leap walls, i.e., overcome every hostile power. ארוּץ, not from רצץ, to smash in pieces, but from רוּץ, to run; construed with the accusative according to the analogy of verbs of motion.
22:31-51 David glorifies God for rescue in the past and for his promises to David’s house for coming generations (see 7:8-16).

22:33 God is my strength and power: and he maketh my way perfect: The doubled praise — *ma'uzzi* (my strength) and *darki tamim* (perfect path). The verbs cast all the king's success on the Lord. David ends his life (cp. ch. 22 sung in the king's old age) attributing every victory not to military skill but to divine sustenance.
22:34 David probably encountered deerduring his many days of hiding out in hilly terrain and caves (1 Sam 19:11–27:12).

22:35 He teacheth my hands to war; so that a bow of steel is broken by mine arms: The Lord's instruction reaches even technique — *yedei lammilkhamah* (hands to war). Bronze (not steel; the Hebrew *nehushah* signals the metal) bows were ornamental, marking the verse's hyperbole — the king's strength stretches beyond the natural by divine empowerment (cp. Ps 18:34).

22:36 36 And Thou reachest me the shield of my salvation, And Thy hearing makes me great. 37 Thou makest my steps broad under me, And my ankles have not trembled. The Lord bestows the true strength for victory in His salvation. The shield of salvation is the shield which consists of salvation, of the helping grace of the Lord. ענתך, for which we find in the psalm ענותך, thy humility, i.e., God's condescending grace, does not mean "thy humiliation," but "thy hearkening," i.e., that practical hearkening on the part of God, when called upon for help, which was manifested in the fact that God made his steps broad, i.e., provided the walker with a broad space for free motion, removing obstructions and stumbling-blocks out of the way.

22:37 Thou hast enlarged my steps under me; so that my feet did not slip: The wide path under the warrior's feet is God's gift of stability. The image — *tahteni* (under me) — names the supporting ground beneath each step. The same metaphor encourages perseverance in the psalter (Ps 18:36; Prov 4:12).

22:38-40 David’s military accomplishments before he became king were impressive (see 1 Sam 17:12-58; 18:17-30; 23:1-5; 30:1-31). Yet rather than exalting himself, David glorified God and gave him the credit.

22:38-40 David’s military accomplishments before he became king were impressive (see 1 Sam 17:12-58; 18:17-30; 23:1-5; 30:1-31). Yet rather than exalting himself, David glorified God and gave him the credit.

22:38-40 David’s military accomplishments before he became king were impressive (see 1 Sam 17:12-58; 18:17-30; 23:1-5; 30:1-31). Yet rather than exalting himself, David glorified God and gave him the credit.

22:41 Thou hast also given me the necks of mine enemies: The bowed neck in ancient warfare signaled subjugation. David ascribes the defeat of opposition not to his sword but to the Lord's giving (*natatah-li*). The same conviction underwrites Paul's later doxology — *not I, but the grace of God* (1 Cor 15:10).

22:42 42 They look out, but there is no deliverer; For Jehovah, but He answereth them not. 43 And I rub in pieces as the dust of the earth, Like the mire of the streets I crush them and stamp upon them. The cry of the foe for help is not attended to; they are annihilated without quarter. ישׁעוּ, to look out to God for help (with אל and על; vid., Isa 17:7-8), is more poetical than ישׁוּעוּ, "they cry" (in the psalm);

22:43 Then did I beat them as small as the dust of the earth: The hyperbole of total triumph — enemies reduced to dust and street-mire. The image, though violent to modern ears, was conventional in royal annals; David's poem nevertheless gives the Lord the agency throughout, refusing self-celebration even in victory.

22:44 44 And Thou rescuest me out of the strivings of my people, Preservest me to be the head of the heathen. People that I knew not serve me. 45 The sons of the stranger dissemble to me, Upon hearsay they obey me. 46 The sons of the stranger despair, And tremble out of their castles. By "the strivings of my people" the more indefinite expression in the psalm, "strivings of the people," is explained. The words refer to the domestic conflicts of David, out of which the Lord delivered him, such as the opposition of Ishbosheth and the rebellions of Absalom and Sheba.

22:45 Strangers shall submit themselves unto me: as soon as they hear, they shall be obedient: The reach of David's reign extends beyond Israel by reputation alone. *Bnei nekhar* (sons of foreign nations) submit by ear, not encounter — a faint type of the Messiah's wider rule, accomplished through proclamation reaching the nations (Rom 10:14-17).

22:46 Strangers shall fade away, and they shall be afraid out of their close places: The corollary — those who do not submit fade. *Wechorgu* (will be afraid) names the dread that drives foreign princes from their strongholds at the news of God's anointed. The verses anticipate the global response to Christ's rule (cp. Ps 2:10-12).

22:47 47 Jehovah liveth, and blessed is my rock, And the God of my refuge of salvation is exalted. 48 The God who giveth me vengeance, And bringeth nations under me; 49 Who leadeth me out from mine enemies, And exalteth me above mine adversaries, Delivereth me from the man of violence. The formula חי־יהוה does not mean "let Jehovah live," for the word יחי would be used for that (vid., 2 Sam 16:16; 1 Sam 10:24), but is a declaration: "the Lord is living." The declaration itself is to be taken as praise of God, for "praising God is simply ascribing to Him the glorious perfections which belong to him;

22:48 It is God that avengeth me, and that bringeth down the people under me: The vengeance belongs to God, not the king (cp. Deut 32:35; Rom 12:19). David positions himself as the vehicle of justice, not its source. The verb *moridem* (bringing down) preserves divine agency at every level of military and political success.

22:49 That bringeth me forth from mine enemies: thou also hast lifted me up: The doubled action — out of enemies' hands, up above their rising. The psalm's larger arc moves from sinking-in-distress (22:5-7) to lifted-on-high (22:49), tracing the rescue pattern God works for his anointed. Paul reads it as fulfilled in Christ (Rom 15:9).

22:50 50 Therefore will I praise Thee, O Jehovah, among the nations, And sing praise to Thy name. 51 As He who magnifies the salvation of His king, And showeth grace to His anointed, To David, and his seed for ever. The grace which the Lord had shown to David was so great, that the praise thereof could not be restricted to the narrow limits of Israel. With the dominion of David over the nations, there spread also the knowledge, and with this the praise, of the Lord who had given him the victory.
